CORE Gateway Payload Specification, Version 2.0

1.2. ··· Domain renewal process: automatic renewal by default
1.3. ··· Automatic domain deletion upon expiry as an option
1.4. ··· General information concerning the API's request/response syntax

1.6. ··· Support for registries requiring TMCH trademark claim notices
1.7. ··· Registry Identifiers
1.8. ··· Command Availability Matrix
1. Preface
1.1. Overview
GatewayNG provides a unified gateway for provisioning domains (and other objects) across a large variety of domain name registries. Managing domains is made possible via its comprehensive web interface (Control Panel) or via a simple key-value based API (CORE Provisioning Protocol, CPP, also known as "Payload") whose requests and responses can be exchanged via different access points. The Payload commands sent via one of GatewayNG's API endpoints are (in most cases) translated to the respective EPP/RRI commands expected by the connected registries, and the EPP/RRI responses from the registries are translated back to Payload responses.
Among other things, managing domains consists of creating them, updating them with different data, renewing them, deleting them, or transferring them from/to other registrars or CORE clients. In addition, GatewayNG provides various convenience functions, such as the automatic creation of required hosts, automatic renewal for all domains by default, comprehensive support for compliance with ICANN policies, statistics and reporting, to name but a few.
The remainder of this document primarily describes GatewayNG's basic functionality and provides details about all requests and responses supported by the CORE Provisioning Protocol ("Payload"). For more information on specific provided services, such as the available API endpoints, IDN support, GatewayNG's DNS provisioning feature or its services for ICANN's WDRP, ERRP, WAP or IRTP policies, please consult the dedicated documents provided in the Control Panel's "Other Documents" submenu of the "Documentation" menu.
1.2. Domain renewal process: automatic renewal by default
Domains in GatewayNG are created with an initial registration period, whose end is marked, generally speaking, by the domain's expiration date. Once this expiration date is reached, the domain is renewed (usually by one year) and its expiration date is moved one year into the future.
Domains can be explicitly renewed via an API renew command or the Control Panel, by a specified number of years. However, this is optional because by default, GatewayNG automatically renews all domains under its management. For registries performing automatic renewals themselves, GatewayNG does so by simply allowing the registry-side renewal to happen and charging the client for the added domain year; for the few registries not performing automatic renewals, GatewayNG does so by explicitly sending a renew command on behalf of the GatewayNG client (which also charges the client for the added domain year). This means that by default, no domain is ever not renewed or deleted by GatewayNG; clients can rest assured that domains in their portfolios will not be lost due to expiry or unsolicited deletions. It also means that, in order to get rid of a domain and its continuous renewal charges, a client must usually send an explicit delete command for the domain.
Most registries offer a so-called "auto-renew grace period" (ARGP for short) which starts after the domain's expiration date and its auto-renewal. During the ARGP (which is 45 days in the case of most gTLDs, but may be significantly shorter or not offered at all for ccTLDs), a GatewayNG client can still delete a domain to get a full refund for the auto-renewal charge (just as CORE will also get a full refund from the registry). Once the ARGP is over, a domain's automatic renewal is final and the auto-renewal charge is non-refundable (both to CORE and the GatewayNG client).
1.3. Automatic domain deletion upon expiry as an option
Certain registrar tools (like the Web Hosting Automation platform "WHMCS") expect domains to be automatically deleted upon expiry unless they are explicitly renewed. Some GatewayNG clients may want to opt out of having their domains automatically renewed for other reasons. In order to facilitate this, GatewayNG offers the option to disable the automatic renewal on a per-domain basis.
If a client does not want a domain to be automatically renewed, the domain's member-autorenew-disabled status can be set (either via the API or the Control Panel).

This is not to be confused with the member-renew-prohibited status, which only prevents explicit renewals by the client, and, in some cases, registry-side auto-renewals. Usage of this status is not recommended.
Once set, the member-autorenew-disabled status will cause GatewayNG to automatically delete the domain just in time before its next auto-renewal charge becomes non-refundable. The feature uses a domain's ARGP (if available) to keep it for some time after the expiration date is reached, in order to give clients and their customers enough time to renew the domain after all. The domain will only be deleted two days before the end of the ARGP, i.e. before the renewal charge would be final. For registries not offering an ARGP, the domain will be deleted two days before its expiration date (because that is when the non-refundable renewal would happen).

If the member-autorenew-disabled is first added while a domain is already in its ARGP, it will not affect the automatic renewal that is already in progress. However, it will take effect for the next automatic renewal. In this situation, the domain must be explicitly deleted for a refund of the renewal charge.
To give clients and their customers ample warning about the imminent deletion, the automatic deletion of the domain by GatewayNG is generally preceded by a so-called "deletion-hold period", during which the domain is merely put on hold (to interrupt its publication in the DNS). GatewayNG adds a deletion-hold status to the domain for this purpose. The duration of this deletion-hold period depends on the duration of the registry's auto-renew grace period and other specifics of the registry, such as whether the registry even supports a hold status (or an equivalent measure). The deletion-hold is shown in a domain inquiry result, and also shown in the Control Panel (along with the date of the domain's scheduled deletion).

For registries not supporting a hold status, GatewayNG will try to employ an equivalent measure to achieve the DNS interruption. If possible, it will remove the name servers from the domain while the deletion-hold status is set. For DENIC, it will set special DNS entries in the domain instead of the name servers as long as the deletion-hold status is present. However, some registries may still not support a hold status at all.
The following rules apply for the deletion-hold period:
  • For registries not supporting a hold status (or any equivalent measure) at all, no deletion-hold period is employed at all, and the domain is deleted without warning two days before the auto-renewal charge becomes non-refundable, i.e. two days before the end of the ARGP, or (if no ARGP is offered) two days before the expiration date.
  • For registries supporting a hold status (or an equivalent measure), the deletion-hold period depends on the ARGP, but shall be at least eight days and at most 20 days:
    • For registries not offering an ARGP, the deletion-hold period is the minimum (eight days).
    • For registries with an ARGP of at least 22 days, the hold period is the maximum (20 days).
    • For registries with an ARGP shorter than 22 days, the hold period is two days shorter than the ARGP, but at least eight days.
Consequently
  • For registries without a hold status, the domain is never put on hold, and deleted two days before the end of the ARGP.
  • For registries with a hold status (or an equivalent measure):
    • For registries not offering an ARGP (which are usually not ICANN-regulated), the domain is put on hold ten days before the expiration date (for eight days), and deleted two days before the expiration date.
    • For registries with a normal ARGP of 45 days (usually ICANN-regulated), the domain is put on hold 23 days after its expiration date (for 20 days), and deleted 43 days after its expiration date.
Right after a domain enters the deletion-hold status, the client will receive a poll message of type domain.change to be informed about the change; that message contains information about the final deletion date of the domain. Likewise, the client will receive another poll message of type domain.change after the domain was finally deleted.

If a domain has already entered the auto-delete procedure (by obtaining the deletion-hold status), any attempt to unset the member-autorenew-disabled status will be rejected with an error message; in order to prevent the deletion in this situation, the domain must be explicitly renewed. Note that this explicit renewal will prevent the imminent deletion, but will not remove the member-autorenew-disabled status (so it can still take effect at the domain's new expiration date).
Domains with the deletion-hold status can still be explicitly deleted to potentially expedite their removal in urgent cases. However, for gTLDs, a pre-deletion ERRP deferred-deletion-hold period of up to eight days may still apply; GatewayNG will however reduce the ERRP hold period by the time the domain had already been in deletion-hold prior to receiving the delete command. See the dedicated ERRP documentation for more information.
Also, domains carrying the deletion-hold status can still be transferred to another registrar (via an external transfer) or to another Gateway NG client (via an internal transfer). Since the losing member is obviously no longer interested in keeping the domain, outgoing transfers (external or internal) for domains in the deletion-hold period are automatically approved by GatewayNG on behalf of the losing client. Likewise, when a domain is already in a pending transfer at the time when GatewayNG finds it being due for its deletion-hold status or its deletion, GatewayNG will also automatically approve the transfer (instead of putting the domain on hold or deleting it, respectively).

When a domain with the member-autorenew-disabled status is transferred internally from one GatewayNG client to another GatewayNG client, the member-autorenew-disabled status is automatically removed from the domain after the successful transfer. The gaining client must set it explicitly again as desired. The deletion-hold status is also removed if the internal transfer happened during the deletion-hold period (see above).
The auto-delete feature described in this section is only available for domains, but not for other renewable/billable object managed in GatewayNG (like GNR e-mail addresses, GNR protection, GNR watch objects, or DNS zones). These objects must always be explicitly deleted to prevent further renewal charges.
1.4. General information concerning the API's request/response syntax
This payload definition specifies the syntax of requests accepted by the CORE gateway via its payload interface and the corresponding responses.
The format consists of key/value pairs, where each pair is given on a separate input/output line of the request/response; key and value are separated by a colon. The following general rules apply:
  • The order in which the lines appear is not important.
  • Whitespace at the beginning of request lines and before and after the key-value separator ':' is allowed and not considered as a part of the key or value.
  • To enable the use of whitespace at the beginning of a value and multi-line fields, the gateway allows using double quotes (") at the beginning and end of the value).
    To enter double quotes within field values specified this way, two double quotes ("") must be used.
  • The gateway reacts to a normal command by sending two responses. The first is an acknowledge (response.type: acknowledge) and is sent immediately after the reception at the server to notify that the request has been received, that it is syntactically correct, that the client authentication was successful, that it conforms so far to the protocol and that it can be queued for processing. It does, however, not give any clues whether the processing of the request will succeed or fail. If one of the conditions above is not satisfied, the command's nominal response is sent back instead, indicating the corresponding error.
    The acknowledge response consists of the following fields:
    Field
    Datatype
    Description
    response.type
    Fixed text, acknowledge.
    Specifies the acknowledge response type.
    response.date
    A date, formatted as yyyy-mm-ddThh:mm:ss.fZ.
    The date and time at which the response has been created.
    payload.version
    Fixed text, 2.0.
    Version of the payload format used in the response.
    transaction.id
    A character string (ASCII subset only).
    The transaction id specified in the request.
  • The responses to all commands may contain billing information concerning the involved account's name, change and balance. However, this information is omitted if the command's execution is free of charge.
  • Most commands require the specification of the involved registry (registry.id).
1.5. Support for internationalized domain names (IDN)
Some registries support domain names that may contain other characters than the well-known set of twenty-six letters, ten digits and the hyphen (LDH). These typically include (but not limited to) accented and extended latin and greek, cyrillic, arabic, hebrew letters, asian ideographs and other scripts.
The payload accepts those domain names in two versions: The so-called presentation form and Punycode encoded. The presentation form is the form in which the domain name is actually displayed. Internally those names are stored in Unicode, but the actual transport mechanism may use a different encoding than Unicode. In this case it is not guaranteed that all characters can be transported and that none is lost, but this issue is out of scope of this document. Punycode, in contrast, is a representation which is used by the DNS system itself and is built upon the LDH set. Unfortunately, the encoding is quite complicated, so it is impossible for a human being to decode or interpret it by hand.
As both variants have their advantages and disadvantages, the payload supports both. In all fields that accept domain names either the presentation form or the Punycode form may be used. In addition, those fields are complemented by an .i15d version. This allows the specification of both variants at the same time – the original field containing the Punycode form and the .i15d field the presentation form. The request is only processed if both fields represent the same domain name. That way, a user can ensure that no error happens due to character conversions and that the Punycode string really represents the desired name.
Similar to the request fields, the response fields are extended by an .i15d field also. The original field contains the Punycode form, while the .i15d field contains the presentation form. Both fields always appear on migrated registries, even on non-IDN names. In this case, both fields contain the same strings, as the Punycode encoding does not touch legacy names.
It should be noted that the chosen solution is the second best only. From a design perspective, it would be better to use the presentation form in the existing fields, as these represent the actual names being registered. In contrast, the Punycode representation is only a workaround to solve deployment problems of IDN in the domain name system and should therefore not be used as the main communication form. As not only the domain name system has to be migrated, but also the whole registration process starting from the registry and ending at the resellers and domain owners, the described solution has been chosen instead, as it has the least impact to those CORE members which are unaware of internationalized domains or are in the process of migration: In the existing fields no new characters will appear; both in requests and responses the new fields can be entirely ignored. Despite the fact that CORE members can perform registrations of IDNs with Punycode encoded names only, they are strongly encouraged to get involved in Unicode, a technology that exists since 1991 and will pervade any computer standard that deals with representations of human language texts. The Unicode standard is maintained by the Unicode Consortium, with their web page at http://www.unicode.org.
1.6. Support for registries requiring TMCH trademark claim notices
GatewayNG offers support for registries which require the submission of information regarding the registrant's acceptance of a trademark claims notice. Such an acceptance may be required if a) a trademark claim for a label matching the domain to be registered is present in the TMCH (Trademark Clearinghouse) and b) the applicable launch phase in the registry involves checking the TMCH for such claims. The registry will usually not permit the registration of the domain unless it is provided with a confirmation that the registrant was presented with a notice containing information about the trademark claim on the domain he is about to register.
GatewayNG supports this procedure by automatically creating a web page presenting the trademark claim (whose URL can be sent to the registrant), allowing the registrant to acknowledge the claim, and submitting acceptance information to the registry when creating the domain.
In such situations, an ordinary domain creation will usually fail with an error message like "a claims notice for the domain is missing". If this happens, the domain must be registered as follows:
  • Execute a domain.check command for the domain in the applicable launch phase. The response indicates the existence of the claim (domain.1.claim.exists: true), the notice ID (domain.1.claim.1.notice.id) and the URL to which you need to send the registrant in order to accept the trademark claim before the domain can be registered (domain.1.claim.1.notice.accept.url).
  • Send the URL from the check response to the registrant, who needs to read the trademark notice there and accept/acknowledge the existence of the claim on the displayed page.
  • Once the registrant has accepted the trademark notice, you will receive a claims.notice.accepted poll message.
  • After the receiving the poll message, you can create the domain via a domain.create command in the applicable launch phase. In order to associate this creation with the accepted trademark notice, make sure the supply the notice ID (as received in the domain.1.claim.1.notice.id field of the domain.check response above) in the claim.1.notice.id field of your domain.create command.
    
    You can also try to create the domain without waiting for the poll message beforehand, however the domain.create command will fail with an error message until the registrant has accepted the trademark claim.
Please refer to the documentation sections dedicated to the domain.check and domain.create commands and the claims.notice.accepted poll message for further details.
